topic: Find memory leaks, orphan process buildup, and process containment failures in system-spec-kit and related cli-X deep-flow orchestration.

focus: Inventory process-spawn entrypoints and background execution patterns in .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit and related cli-X deep-flow orchestration
noveltyJustification: First iteration produced a full 25-callsite inventory across audited dispatch, raw-shell dispatch, sidecar lifecycle, IPC bridge, hook shims, matrix runner, and stress harness; no prior state to overlap against.
keyQuestions: Which system-spec-kit command, MCP, or memory paths can spawn long-lived child processes without cleanup?
Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
Which sidecars or helpers are expected daemons versus unexpected leftovers after an iteration?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: Which system-spec-kit command, MCP, or memory paths can spawn long-lived child processes without cleanup?
Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
Which sidecars or helpers are expected daemons versus unexpected leftovers after an iteration?
ruledOut: nohup / setsid / disown / trailing-& backgrounding inside scripts/ (zero matches)
LEAF deep-research and deep-review agents as a spawn multiplier (HARD BLOCK forbids sub-agent dispatch)

focus: State-machine, advisory-lock, reducer, and post-dispatch failure-recovery audit
noveltyJustification: Pivoted off iteration 001's spawn-inventory framing into the state-machine and recovery surfaces: heredoc executor field-name mismatch, marker-scan template self-trigger, iteration_start vs previousStateLogSize off-by-one, reducer hard-crash on missing files, advisory-lock liveness gap, and stuck-recovery scratch-file accumulation are all net-new and not derivable from iteration 001.
keyQuestions: Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
ruledOut: Re-running iteration 001's spawn-inventory framing — closed scope, near-zero new-info ratio
permissions-gate.ts as an in-scope failure-recovery surface — orphaned from commands/spec_kit/ and the deep-loop dispatch path

focus: Deep-review, AI council, and multi-agent command process / lock / cleanup audit
noveltyJustification: Net-new cross-flow findings: deep-review has zero advisory-lock infrastructure (deep-research has 10 hits, deep-review zero); ai-council writeStateJsonl truncates a documented append-only log; rotateIfNeeded single-step rotates with no ladder; improve:agent has no automated archive and halts on any pre-existing runtime; complete :with-research nests the full deep-research lock-acquire path; agent vs SKILL permission drift (bash deny vs Bash allowed); per-iter cli-devin scratch files accumulate without rotation. The iter-002 deep-loop defects are confirmed to cross-apply to deep-review at specific YAML lines.
keyQuestions: Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: Deep-review ships with no advisory lock; cross-flow concurrent runs have no serialization (Findings 1-3)
The iter-002 deep-loop defects cross-apply to deep-review verbatim (Findings 4-6)
Per-iter cli-devin artifacts accumulate in deep-review with no rotation (Finding 7)
ai-council violates its own append-only contract via two paths: writeStateJsonl truncation and rotateIfNeeded single-step rotate (Findings 10-14)
improve:agent has strict single-shot policy with no automated archive (Finding 17)
complete :with-research embeds the full deep-research lock-acquire path (Findings 21-22)
Cross-flow memory-write race exists between deep-review, deep-research, ai-council, and improve (Findings 24-25)
ruledOut: Re-running iter-001 spawn-inventory framing against council/improve assets — near-zero new-info ratio
YAML-runtime timeout_seconds reverse-engineering — out of system-spec-kit scope
permissions-gate.ts as in-scope — iter-002 already established orphaning

focus: cli-X self-invocation guards, nested routing rules, and cross-CLI loop risks
noveltyJustification: Net-new cross-flow audit: cli-codex/cli-gemini lack the Bash one-liner guard their three siblings emit; runAuditedExecutorCommand has zero self-invocation enforcement; ExecutorNotWiredError is dead code while cli-claude-code/cli-gemini YAML branches are live; runtime-detection.ts lacks an OpenCode branch and disagrees with cli-claude-code on CLAUDECODE vs CLAUDE_CODE; per-skill guards check only their own runtime signatures so a two-hop cross-CLI chain slips through; the deep-flow exception removes per-iteration guard re-checks; the five legitimate-exception families (parallel-detached, cloud-handoff, none, none, none) are asymmetric across the family.
keyQuestions: Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
answeredQuestions: Self-invocation guards live as Markdown pseudocode and per-skill Bash prerequisite lines; never enforced at the dispatch wrapper (Findings 1-3)
cli-codex and cli-gemini SKILL.md are missing the Bash one-liner guard their three siblings ship (Finding 4)
cli-claude-code SKILL.md uses CLAUDECODE; runtime-detection.ts uses CLAUDE_CODE/CLAUDE_SESSION_ID; OpenCode is not classified by runtime-detection.ts at all (Findings 5-6)
ExecutorNotWiredError class exists but is never thrown; cli-claude-code and cli-gemini are actively wired in the YAML (Finding 7)
record_executor_audit writes type: across all five cli-X branches while the schema expects kind: (Finding 8 generalizes iter-002 #1-4)
Per-skill guards check only their own provider tokens; a two-hop cross-CLI chain (claude->codex->claude) trips no single guard (Finding 9)
The deep-flow session exception explicitly removes per-iteration self-invocation re-checks (Finding 10)
cli-opencode permits parallel-detached, cli-devin permits cloud-handoff, the other three have no exception (Finding 11)

Agent-path drift (.claude/agents vs .opencode/agents in cli-claude-code), agent-side bash:allow with no shell-out prohibition (deep-research), and depth-by-absence detection (ai-council) bypass the runtime guards (Findings 12, 14, 15)
ruledOut: Re-running iter-001 spawn inventory framing across the five cli-X skills
Inspecting actual binary semantics of claude/codex/opencode/devin/gemini
Re-checking ExecutorNotWiredError for cli-opencode/cli-devin

focus: Apple Silicon swap, wired memory, sidecar, reranker, embedder, Ollama, and expected-daemon classification in skill + spec-kit docs
noveltyJustification: Net-new classification audit: no runtime swap/wired-memory preflight exists anywhere; preflightOverride.swapPreflightBypassed is dead metadata with no code consumer; 'orphan' is exclusively in-DB language across system-spec-kit; no canonical expected-long-lived-daemon allowlist; rerank sidecar's RAM-Budget note is the only swap mention and never explains MPS-wired observability; embedder Node-fork sidecar inherits full parent env while rerank sidecar uses an explicit allowlist (opposite security models, undocumented asymmetry); ensure_rerank_sidecar.py never reaps on SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER flip-off, so the sidecar persists ~1.5GB indefinitely; embedder sidecar self-evict on SPECKIT_EMBEDDER_SIDECAR_IDLE_MS is silent SIGTERM with no operator-facing doc; execution-router.ts process.once handlers do not run on kill -9 to MCP parent; cache_byte caps in ENV_REFERENCE.md cover user-process RSS only and are not disambiguated from MPS-wired/Ollama RSS; memory_health exposes Node embedder sidecar PID only, not rerank/Ollama/launcher/ccc-run-daemon; cocoindex DOC-334/335/336 daemon classifier is the only model and has no sibling for the other four daemons.
keyQuestions: Which sidecars or helpers are expected daemons versus unexpected leftovers after an iteration?
Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: No runtime swap/wired-memory preflight gate exists anywhere in audit scope (Finding 1, 2)
No canonical 'expected long-lived daemon' allowlist in repo; classification scattered across 5 docs + 1 binary helper (Finding 4, 13, 23)
Apple Silicon MPS-wired memory is never explained in any audit-scope doc despite being a load-bearing failure mode (Finding 6)
Cache byte caps bound user-process RSS only; doc never disambiguates from MPS-wired/Ollama RAM (Finding 7, 15)
Embedder Node-fork sidecar self-evicts at SPECKIT_EMBEDDER_SIDECAR_IDLE_MS default 300s with silent SIGTERM, no operator-facing note (Finding 8)
Rerank sidecar persists ~1.5GB across SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER flip-off because ensure_rerank_sidecar.py uses skip_if_disabled=True without reaping (Finding 10)
Embedder Node-fork sidecar inherits FULL parent MCP env; rerank Python sidecar uses an explicit allowlist — opposite security models documented nowhere (Finding 12)
kill -9 of MCP parent can orphan embedder Node-fork because process.once shutdown hooks do not run (Finding 18)
Ollama lifecycle is fully external; no audit-scope doc declares it an expected long-lived service (Finding 13)
preflightOverride.swapPreflightBypassed + recordedSwapUsage + recordedFreePages in this packet's config are write-only operator notes with no telemetry consumer (Finding 2, 20)
DOC-334/335/336 cocoindex daemon classifier is the only one; no sibling for rerank sidecar, mk-spec-memory daemon, embedder sidecar, or Ollama (Finding 24)
Arc 008 phase 005 HOLD verdict cites p95 latency only; SKILL.md §RAM-Budget cites host swap pressure; the two reasons not reconciled in a single decision record (Finding 21)
ruledOut: Re-running iter-001 spawn inventory through sidecar/embedder lens
Inspecting macOS vm_stat / sysctl vm.swapusage / memory_pressure binaries
Reading cocoindex ccc run-daemon supervisor binary
Re-checking deep-loop/deep-review YAML for advisory-lock + reducer defects (closed in iter-002/003)
Cross-CLI self-invocation guards + runtime-detection asymmetries (closed in iter-004)

focus: Independently map process owners, spawned command patterns, cleanup gaps, and long-lived helper responsibilities across system-spec-kit and cli-X paths
noveltyJustification: This pass converted prior spawn inventories into a source-cited ownership map, identifying wrapper-versus-raw-shell asymmetry, kind/type executor drift, doc-only kill-between-iteration ownership, and helper-specific lifecycle boundaries.
keyQuestions: Which system-spec-kit command, MCP, or memory paths can spawn long-lived child processes without cleanup?
Which deep-research, deep-review, council, or cli-X workflows can produce nested CLI process storms?
Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
Which sidecars or helpers are expected daemons versus unexpected leftovers after an iteration?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: Deep-research artifact and dispatch ownership is workflow-owned, with the LEAF agent scoped to packet-local iteration outputs.
Deep-loop CLI execution is asymmetric: codex/gemini/claude use runAuditedExecutorCommand in deep-research, while opencode/devin and most deep-review branches remain raw shell snippets.
cli-X cleanup is primarily a documented caller/operator responsibility via single-dispatch and SIGKILL rules, not one shared enforced supervisor.
Embedder sidecar, CocoIndex daemon probe, hook shims, Claude autosave, matrix runner, and git/session extractors each have separate lifecycle owners and cleanup semantics.
ruledOut: Re-running the full iteration-001 spawn inventory; this iteration focused on ownership and cleanup responsibility rather than callsite counts.
Inspecting secrets, auth files, token stores, or environment dumps; not needed and forbidden by the iteration contract.
Launching nested cli-X processes or sub-agents; explicitly forbidden by the iteration contract.
Using node_modules, changelogs, manual playbooks, and tests as primary runtime evidence after initial broad search noise.

focus: Inspect MCP server, context server, memory save/search/index handlers, reducer scripts, background timers, caches, retry jobs, and file handles for long-lived resources that can accumulate or keep processes alive unexpectedly.
noveltyJustification: This pass moved below process spawning into the MCP process lifetime itself: scan-lease cleanup, scheduled graph-refresh timers, singleton Tier 3 routing cache, retrieval-session accumulators, cursor retention, file-watcher maps, ingest queue backlog, retry shutdown cancellation, and search-audit rotation are new in-process resource findings not covered by the prior spawn/sidecar/ownership iterations.
keyQuestions: Which in-process timers, caches, queues, and leases can keep the MCP server alive or grow across requests?
Which memory save/search/index paths miss explicit bounds or shutdown cleanup?
Which apparent file-handle risks are already closed or bounded?
answeredQuestions: memory_index_scan can leave scan_started_at active until stale-lease expiry if an error occurs after lease acquisition and before explicit completion.
Scheduled graph refresh has a cancellable timer, but context-server does not call the cancel function during shutdown and the timer is not unrefed.
Tier 3 routing cache, retrieval session state, file watcher path maps, ingest pendingQueue, and retry-manager in-flight work have process-local accumulation or liveness risks.

Tool cache, embedding cache, direct spec-level file reads, and the deep-research reducer have visible caps, finally blocks, or one-shot execution that make them unlikely primary process-keeping leaks.
ruledOut: memory-index-discovery.detectSpecLevel openSync path: closes fd in finally
tool-cache as an unbounded timer/cache leak: max entries, aux caps, unrefed cleanup interval, and shutdown are present
embedding-cache as unbounded cache growth: byte/profile/query budgets and eviction are present
deep-research reducer as a long-lived resource: one-shot synchronous CLI path exits

focus: Inspect ccc search, reranker sidecar, Ollama, embedder backends, gtimeout, browser/devtools helpers, and external MCP tooling for leftover process patterns or cleanup blind spots relevant to system-spec-kit deep workflows.
noveltyJustification: This pass adds process-boundary evidence for ccc search daemon persistence, CocoIndex-default rerank sidecar spawning, Code Mode MCP session close gaps, browser bdg cleanup, and embedder sidecar request timeouts beyond the prior broad sidecar and in-process cache iterations.
keyQuestions: Which sidecars or helpers are expected daemons versus unexpected leftovers after an iteration?
Where are timeout handling, signal handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
What verification would prove each proposed fix actually reduces process buildup or memory pressure?
answeredQuestions: ccc search can start a detached ccc run-daemon process that is not covered by cli-X cleanup strings that only target ccc search.
CocoIndex MCP can auto-spawn the rerank sidecar by default through COCOINDEX_RERANK_VIA_SIDECAR even when SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER is false.
Ollama is an external HTTP service in the inspected code, not a child process spawned by system-spec-kit.
Code Mode external MCP sessions have an explicit close path in @utcp/mcp, but the wrapper does not call it on shutdown.
bdg browser sessions have a documented Chrome leak if bdg stop is skipped.
ruledOut: Ollama as a system-spec-kit-spawned child process
Duplicate CocoIndex daemon races as the primary current risk
Rerank sidecar final uvicorn process inheriting the full parent environment

focus: Inspect stale locks, pause files, interrupted-session recovery, cancellation paths, signal handling, JSONL partial write recovery, and resume/restart behavior for leak or duplicate-dispatch hazards.
noveltyJustification: This pass adds new recovery-contract evidence around stale lock metadata absence, between-iteration-only pause semantics, non-atomic JSONL append/rewrite behavior, delta recovery not feeding canonical state, and restart lifecycle schema drift; several timeout and wrapper details partially overlap earlier iterations.
keyQuestions: Where are self-invocation guards, timeout handling, signal handling, stale-lock handling, and cleanup traps missing or inconsistent?
What recovery paths exist for interrupted sessions, pause sentinels, cancellation, and JSONL partial writes?
What resume/restart behavior can create leak or duplicate-dispatch hazards?
answeredQuestions: Stale-lock handling is policy-level and lacks owner PID, TTL, heartbeat, or automatic stale-lock reap in the inspected workflow contract.
Pause sentinel handling is between-iteration only and does not cancel an already-running CLI executor.
The audited CLI wrapper records timeout/crash/signal outcomes but does not install parent signal handlers or kill a process group.
JSONL recovery has doc/code drift: docs promise tolerant skip or reconstruction, while the reducer throws by default unless lenient mode is selected.
Delta files are validated but are only consumed for resource-map emission, not canonical iteration-state repair.
Restart lifecycle events omit continuedFromRun even though the lifecycle contract requires it.
ruledOut: Re-running spawn inventory, sidecar, Ollama, ccc daemon, browser/devtools, and in-process cache hunts covered by previous iterations.
Launching nested CLI executors or sub-agents.
Inspecting secrets, auth files, token stores, or environment dumps.
Treating the current packet as actively paused or locked; current sentinel checks found neither file.

focus: Synthesize the leak taxonomy and remediation backlog from iterations 001-009
noveltyJustification: This iteration adds cross-iteration ownership, severity, and verification structure rather than new source-code discoveries.
keyQuestions: Which leak classes recur across iterations 001-009?
Which remediation candidates have source evidence, finding class, owner, severity, and verification strategy?
Are any earlier iteration artifacts missing?
answeredQuestions: The recurring taxonomy is process containment, recursive dispatch containment, lock/state integrity, daemon classification, sidecar lifecycle, in-process retention, external-tool cleanup, cross-flow concurrency, and host-memory observability.
The synthesis produced 13 remediation candidates plus an artifact-completeness finding, each with evidence, class, likely owner, severity, and verification strategy.
No prior iteration artifacts were missing.
ruledOut: Missing earlier iteration artifacts as an evidence blocker
Live process-table reproduction during this synthesis-only iteration
Runtime implementation fixes during research
